title: The End of Anarchy Is Drawing Near!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. The source pages confirm several OCR errors in the derived text, including `டோட்டியிட` for `போட்டியிட`, `தோரதல்` for `தேர்தல்`, `நானகு` for `நான்கு`, broken word spacing, and corrupted quotation marks. The standalone line `வ ப ப 0 கதத` is not printed source text; it is an OCR artifact inserted at a page break and is omitted. The printed salutation is also restored. These repairs are confined to the derived Tamil reading copy. The canonical source files remain unchanged.
paragraphs: Every newspaper today has reported that the Supreme Court has issued notice to former Chief Minister Jayalalithaa in the case concerning her contesting from four constituencies in the 2001 election, even though it was already known that no person could contest from more than two constituencies in a single election.

Just as the disproportionate-assets case against Jayalalithaa has a fourteen-year history, and the case concerning her depositing a birthday gift into her personal account while serving as Chief Minister has a thirteen-year history, the case over her contesting from four constituencies has likewise become a once-in-twelve-years “Mahamaham” case with a twelve-year history.

Since twelve years have passed, let me briefly remind you of the details. In the 2001 election, Jayalalithaa filed nomination papers in four constituencies with the intention of gaining public sympathy, and all four nominations were rejected. The filing of nominations began on 16 April 2001. That very day, Jayalalithaa submitted her nomination for the Krishnagiri constituency to Returning Officer M. Mathivanan. On 18 April, she submitted her nomination for the Andipatti constituency to the Returning Officer named Jaya. On 23 April, the final day for filing nominations, papers were also submitted on Jayalalithaa's behalf in the Bhuvanagiri and Pudukkottai constituencies.

Scrutiny of the nominations took place on the 24th. After examining Jayalalithaa's nomination, Krishnagiri Returning Officer Mathivanan announced that it was being rejected because she had been sentenced to more than two years' imprisonment and had filed nominations in more than two constituencies. Jayalalithaa had been sentenced to three years' imprisonment in the TANSI land-deal case. Section 8 of the Representation of the People Act says that a person sentenced to imprisonment for more than two years cannot contest an election. Under Section 8 of the Representation of the People Act, 1951, a person convicted by a court and sentenced to imprisonment for not less than two years is disqualified from contesting elections from the date of conviction. The disqualification continues for a further six years after release from prison. This was the Election Commission's rule, issued in a circular by the Election Commission of India in 1997.

Siddhartha Shankar Ray, former Chief Minister of West Bengal and a distinguished lawyer, appeared for Jayalalithaa and argued that the High Court had stayed her prison sentence and that her nomination should therefore be accepted. But relying on the Representation of the People Act and the Election Commission's 1997 circular, the Returning Officer rejected her nomination. Her nomination was rejected in Andipatti on the same grounds. The nominations in Bhuvanagiri and Pudukkottai were rejected because she had filed papers in more than two constituencies.
The relevant election rule stated:

> If it is found at the time of scrutiny that a candidate has been nominated from more than two constituencies of the same class of a general election or the simultaneous by-elections, his nomination paper will be rejected. A candidate who has been nominated from more than two constituencies will also be guilty of making a false declaration that in his nomination paper which contains a categorical declaration that he has not been nominated from more than two constituencies.

That is, when nominations are scrutinised, if a candidate has filed nominations in more than two constituencies in a general election or simultaneous by-elections, the nomination will be rejected. A person filing in more than two constituencies is also liable to the charge of acting contrary to the categorical declaration in the nomination paper that the candidate has not been nominated from more than two constituencies.
The editorials published by *The Indian Express* and *The Hindu* on 26 April 2001 about the rejection of Jayalalithaa's nominations deserve particular attention.
*The Indian Express* wrote:

> There is nothing surprising about the rejection of all four nomination papers filed by AIADMK leader Jayalalithaa. The Returning Officers have strictly followed the Election Commission's 1997 circular directing that a person sentenced to more than two years cannot contest an election. Although the law prohibited filing nominations in more than two constituencies, Jayalalithaa tried her luck in four. Jayalalithaa, who does not respect public opinion, calculated that she could come to power on the wave of sympathy generated by the rejection of her nominations.

Yet during her election campaign, Jayalalithaa claimed that the DMK was responsible for the rejection of her nominations and that she had been prevented from contesting. She sought public sympathy and diverted the people; as a result, the AIADMK won.
In its editorial dated 6 May 2001, the weekly *Ananda Vikatan* wrote:

> Jayalalithaa's approach in this election has violated every principle of justice and fairness. Even after her nominations were rejected, and even after she lost her eligibility to be elected to the Legislative Assembly, she does not appear to have changed her plan. Everywhere she goes, she proclaims that if the AIADMK alliance wins, she herself will be Chief Minister. It is painful that, thinking the people know nothing about political conventions, she has dared to fool them by calculating that they will vote only if she projects herself as Chief Minister. Once she was barred from contesting, it would have been acceptable had Jayalalithaa wholeheartedly accepted that ruling and said, “Make our alliance victorious. If you do so, I will appoint a leader acceptable to you as Chief Minister on my behalf and strive to provide good government.”

On the 14th, while the election results were still coming in, a meeting of AIADMK legislators was convened in great haste. Jayalalithaa was elected leader of the legislature party. That very evening, Governor Fathima Beevi hurriedly administered the oath of office to her. Newspapers at the time said the swearing-in had been rushed through out of fear that someone might file a case seeking to prevent her from assuming office.

In an editorial on 15 May 2001, *The Hindu* wrote that this was the first time a person categorically barred from contesting an Assembly election had become Chief Minister, and also the first time a person convicted by a court had occupied such an office. On both counts, it said, the action violated the fundamental spirit of the Constitution and raised painful questions in law and justice. Several prominent English newspapers also wrote that a person convicted on corruption charges had violated moral standards by taking office as Chief Minister.

When it was suggested that a case might be filed against the Governor for administering the oath in such haste, Jayalalithaa said, “The Governor's actions cannot be challenged in court; no one can question the Governor's decision.” But it should be remembered that when the then Governor, Channa Reddy, granted permission in 1995 to prosecute Jayalalithaa in the TANSI corruption case, she filed cases against the Governor in both the High Court and the Supreme Court. Not only that, she also had the Assembly pass a resolution declaring: “This House calls upon the Union government immediately to recall Governor Channa Reddy.”

As early as 24 April 2001, C. Kuppusamy wrote to the Election Commission requesting criminal proceedings against Jayalalithaa for filing more than two nominations and falsely declaring that she had not done so. After the election, Kuppusamy and four Members of Parliament asked the Election Commission to prosecute her. On 9 August 2001, the Election Commission instructed Tamil Nadu's Chief Electoral Officer to examine the nominations and file a case under Section 177 of the Indian Penal Code.
On 8 March 2002, C. Kuppusamy filed a writ petition in the Madras High Court seeking appropriate action by the Election Commission on the matter.

When the case came before the Supreme Court in 2012, the judges heard arguments from both sides and asked: “When the law says that a person contesting an election must not file nominations in more than two constituencies, why did Jayalalithaa file nominations in four? Why did a former Chief Minister do so? It was wrong for a party leader, a person holding public office, to file nominations in this way. Was this conduct fair? Was it right?”

When the matter again came before the Supreme Court on 21 December 2012, counsel Lalit, appearing for Jayalalithaa, argued that because the Election Commission had rejected her nominations, the Supreme Court could not intervene in the case. The judges interrupted him, observing: “The Returning Officers may have been admirers of Jayalalithaa and may on that basis have decided that no action was required.” They also asked: “Does being a person in public life permit Jayalalithaa to violate election rules? Should the law remain silent?”

Finally, the judges ruled: “Because the Madras High Court did not properly examine the reports submitted by the Returning Officers of Bhuvanagiri and Pudukkottai, the High Court itself must reconsider the matter on the basis of those reports and take appropriate action.” They also stated that the offence was punishable by six months' imprisonment.

While the matter was being heard in the Madras High Court on this basis, C. Kuppusamy, who had initiated the case, died. Citing his death as a reason, the High Court dismissed the case. Although Kuppusamy had died, A.K.S. Vijayan, one of the four other Members of Parliament who had brought the case, appealed to the Supreme Court, arguing that the dismissal was wrong.

It was this appeal that came up yesterday before a Supreme Court bench comprising Justices H.L. Dattu and Madan Lokur. Senior advocate Andhyarjuna, appearing for A.K.S. Vijayan, argued: “A public-interest case brought by a person cannot be dismissed merely because that person has died.” He also pointed out that A.K.S. Vijayan had joined C. Kuppusamy at the time in lodging the complaint with the election authorities.
Accepting these submissions, the Supreme Court judges ordered that notice be issued to Jayalalithaa and the Election Commission, calling upon them to respond.

Those who themselves face cases dragged out like a twelve-year “Mahamaham” now act in the arrogance of holding office, filing defamation cases against opposition members at every turn and trying to drag them through the courts. They say they are ready to appear in court, yet when others attempt to do the same, they try to obstruct even that.

For example, yesterday, when party Treasurer and younger brother M.K. Stalin came to Dindigul to appear in a defamation case, party comrades put up several flex boards to welcome him. Unable to tolerate even this, AIADMK members went as far as the court, brutally attacked and injured party lawyers and members of the public, and the news appeared with photographs in every newspaper. Those in power must not forget that if they imagine they can swagger about in the arrogance of office and indulge in lawless violence, the day that will put an end to it is drawing near!
With affection,
M.K.
